nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on shallow coral reefs and rocky substrates across the tropical Indo-Pacific, with the aquarium trade sourcing animals primarily from the Marshall Islands. Occupies abandoned gastropod (e.g. snail or triton) shells and shelters in rockwork and crevices between foraging bouts.

decorPreferences: live rock with abundant caves and crevices for hiding
sand or fine rubble substrate for sifting and burrowing
an assortment of empty gastropod shells of progressively larger sizes
mature reef tank with stable parameters
secure rockwork (animals will dislodge unstable pieces while foraging)

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied omnivorous diet of nuisance algae (green hair algae, cyanobacteria), detritus, leftover fish food, dried seaweed/Nori, frozen mysis or brine shrimp, and small pieces of raw shrimp or fish. In well-fed aquaria with abundant algae, supplemental feeding only once or twice a week is commonly suggested; supplemental meaty food is more important once the available algae is depleted to discourage predation on snails and tubeworms.

goodWith: peaceful reef fish (gobies, blennies, cardinalfish, small wrasses)
most LPS, SPS, soft corals, and zoanthids
other small reef-safe hermits and shrimp of similar size
larger snails with shells the crab cannot fit into
avoidWith: small or thin-shelled snails (commonly killed for their shells)
feather duster worms and small tubeworms (have been reported eaten)
very small ornamental shrimp and tiny inverts (predation risk when dense)
predatory wrasses, triggerfish, large puffers, and large hawkfish

summary: A mid-sized Indo-Pacific ornamental hermit crab with electric blue and black banded legs and bright orange antennae. Generally considered a valuable cleanup-crew member that grazes hair algae and cyanobacteria.
careNotes: Calcinus elegans is a small-to-mid-sized marine hermit crab in the family Diogenidae, native to the tropical Indo-Pacific and most commonly collected for the aquarium trade from the Marshall Islands. Adults typically reach about 2-5 cm including the shell; LiveAquaria and BlueZoo both cite a maximum of roughly 2 inches (~5 cm), while Tank Facts cites a smaller 0.75-1.5 inch range, so an averaged range is used here. The legs are alternately banded electric blue and black, the eyestalks are blue, and the antennae and mouthparts are bright orange — making it one of the more visually striking ornamental hermits in the hobby. It is a useful cleanup-crew species that grazes filamentous green hair algae and cyanobacteria and sifts sand for detritus, contributing to substrate aeration. Standard reef parameters apply (22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.025, dKH 8-12). Highly intolerant of copper-based medications — Tank Facts notes the species 'does not tolerate any type of metal traces in the water'. Reef-safe in the usual hobby sense (will not damage corals), but classified here as 'with caution' because the species is well-documented as predatory toward small snails (often killing them to take their shells) and small tubeworms, and may pick at sessile inverts in densely stocked tanks. Provide an assortment of progressively larger empty gastropod shells to reduce snail predation and to allow growth between molts. Generally peaceful toward fish and most invertebrates of similar or larger size, but can squabble with conspecifics over shells in small tanks. Sources disagree on temperament — LiveAquaria rates it 'peaceful' while BlueZoo rates it 'semi-aggressive'; given documented snail predation and shell competition, semi-aggressive is used here as the averaged value. Minimum tank size of around 75 L (~20 US gal) is recommended; BlueZoo's 24-gallon (~90 L) floor is a slightly more cautious alternative.
breedingNotes: Generally considered not bred in captivity; the trade is essentially entirely wild-caught from the Indo-Pacific. Like most marine hermits, Calcinus elegans has a planktonic larval phase (zoea) that requires open-water rearing conditions not achievable in a typical display tank. Sources do not document hobbyist-scale captive breeding.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters (72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025), care level ('Easy'), maximum size (~2 in), diet (omnivore), temperament ('peaceful'), reef compatibility ('Yes'), and Marshall Islands origin.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics — Electric Blue Knuckle Hermit Crab
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (~2 in), minimum tank size (24 gal ~ 90 L), care level ('Beginner'), reef compatibility ('Yes'), and temperament ('semi-aggressive'). Also documented broader Indo-Pacific / Hawaii / Marshall Islands distribution and the trade synonym 'Neon Blue Leg Hermit Crab'.

additional.name: Tank Facts — Electric Blue Hermit Crab
additional.notes: Used to cross-check family (Diogenidae), water parameters (76-80°F, pH 7.8-8.4, SG 1.023-1.026), reef safety ('Yes'), copper intolerance ('does not tolerate any type of metal traces'), and Marshall Islands origin. Smaller size citation (0.75-1.5 in) factored into averaged adultSizeCm range.

additional.name: SaltwaterAquarium.com — Electric Blue Hermit Crab (Calcinus elegans)
additional.notes: Third secondary used to cross-check diet (scavenger/detritivore feeding on green hair algae and cyanobacteria), reef safety, behavior (snail-shell predation and molting risk), and the need for an assortment of suitable empty shells.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Sources do not give a typical molt interval. Adequate iodine, calcium, and trace elements are commonly cited as important for proper molting; molts are generally left in the tank to be consumed for calcium reuptake. Animals are vulnerable to predation by tankmates and to shell theft by other hermits in the hours immediately after molting.
crustacean.breedingNotes: No reliably reported captive-breeding success in the home-aquarium literature. The species has a planktonic larval phase that is not practical to rear in a display tank.
